vector_chunks.text:
Special activities for 28052025
Some external hard drives are missing - we don’t know where they are. Let’s find them all and put the information here: Storage units taks and comments in the subtable “Hard disks”. 
In the instruction document below we provided a file structure example, try to organise your project in this way. 
General activities
Remove as much unnecessary data as possible from your computer, hard drive, P-drive, L-drive, cpu1-data/ (see the next guidelines).
Update in the Wiki inventory if you are using currently a new hard drive or computer: https://wiki.helsinki.fi/xwiki/bin/view/FL/Farkkila%20Lab/IT%20Infrastructure/Inventory/
Re-organize your data in a more efficient way if possible.
Compress all raw and intermediate data from the Cold storage disks, even if it's not yours.
Detect unused files owned by others in your workstation or laptop, CSC. Backup those files or delete them, consult this with the project managers.
Write README files for each folder where you have stored your data.
Guidelines about data organization
Identify in your project which files are raw data, intermediate files, and result files. Then make a folder to store each type of files. Example of type of files:
Raw Files: Files generated by dedicated technologies, example: RCPNL, FASTQ, FLOW. These files are not needed anymore after generating relevant intermediate files. These files after a point can be removed, especially if your project is about to conclude and you have generated relevant intermediate files.
First milestone intermediate files: Files directly generated after processing Raw files. Those are used for subsequent analysis. Example: OME.tif, BAM, CSV tables. Particularly those files will replace the need to use Raw files.

intermediate files.
First milestone intermediate files: Files directly generated after processing Raw files. Those are used for subsequent analysis. Example: OME.tif, BAM, CSV tables. Particularly those files will replace the need to use Raw files.
Milestone intermediate files: Files that after heavy computation (several days), or a super-long manual data curation are used for relevant subsequent analysis. These files are mainly produced using the first milestone intermediate file. Remove other intermediate files that are not milestones.
Result Files: Published results, key visualizations for presentations, data summaries to share with collaborators.
project_name/
├── README.txt
├── notebooks/ # Jupyter or R notebooks
│ └── analysis.ipynb
├── raw_data/ # Raw files (temporary, can be deleted later)
│ ├── sample1.fastq
│ ├── sample2.fastq
│ ├── experiment1.rcpnl
│ └── flowdata.fcs
├── intermediate_data/
│ ├── first_milestone/ # direct output from raw files
│ │ ├── sample1_ome.tif
│ │ ├── sample2_ome.tif
│ │ ├── alignments.bam
│ │ └── expression_matrix.csv
│ └── milestone/ # Heavy computation or manual curation
│ ├── curated_annotations.csv
│ ├── dimensionality_reduction_results.h5ad
│ └── spatial_features.parquet
├── results/ # Final results, visualizations, summary tables
│ ├── figures/
│ │ ├── fig1_UMAP.png
│ │ └── fig2_survival_curve.pdf
│ ├── tables/
│ │ └── final_summary_table.xlsx
│ └── presentations/
│ └── group_meeting_results.pptx
└── scripts/ # Scripts for preprocessing, analysis, visualization
 ├── preprocess_raw.py
 ├── analysis_pipeline.R
 └── plot_results.py
Identify redundant files:
Typical redundant files: Result1, Result_final, Result_the_one. Keep the most updated, remove all other versions.

or preprocessing, analysis, visualization
 ├── preprocess_raw.py
 ├── analysis_pipeline.R
 └── plot_results.py
Identify redundant files:
Typical redundant files: Result1, Result_final, Result_the_one. Keep the most updated, remove all other versions. Use dates to differentiate which version is the most updated, example: Results_2811204. Save your scripts in https://github.com/farkkilab to re-generate any of the other results versions if needed. 
Remove all intermediate files which can be easily re-generated using your scripts.
Remove data from experiments that did not work and are probably will never be missed, specially if you already have result files.

Add a README in each folder to describe what each file is, if even you can not remember what that file is, then maybe it is not needed anymore, delete it!

Organize and Standardize File Naming
Implement a consistent naming convention including:
Project name, experiment ID, and file type (e.g., raw, intermediate/Spatial_analysis, intermediate/Quantification, intermediate/Cell_visualization, others, Results/Microscopy_screenshots).
Date format (e.g., YYYY-MM-DD) and versioning for easy identification.
Give a meaningful long name to your files. Cell_quantification_2pixels_myleoid_cells_28112024.csv
Cell_quantification_2pixels_lymphoid_cells_28112024.csv

vector_chunks.source_file: Cleaning day 251205/Cleaning day 251205 external drive listing.xlsx

vector_chunks.text:
Dry lab cleaning day tasks
The main aim for the dry lab cleaning should be to map and prepare data for transfer to Databank/Allas - if you have large files stored on physical hard drives, or the P-drives (or the L-drive), please see task 3
Databank can store pseudonymized data in large quantities, i.e. tCycIF data. If your project has raw tCycIF data that needs to be stored, prioritize preparing that data for transfer in the cleaning day
To do list to follow:
Dry lab cleaning day personal to do list
Tasks
1 Physically clean your physical domain i.e. desk
2 Inventory data cables and USB hubs Return ALL DATA CABLES and USB hubs not in active use- return to Deb. Send info about cables and hubs in your possession to Deb, emphasis on USB-A/USB-C cables and multiport USB hubs
3 Google Drive: Revise your project status - archive inactive projects into the Projects > ARCHIVE subfolder
Project log updates!
4 Update the list of external hard drives, their owners, contents, and space - To facilitate transfer of data to Allas/Databank ch
4.1 Specific Guidelines for external Hard-disks: 
In the above document, go through all of your external disks 
Under the “Data” column, add a general description of data. For the “Type” column, mark either active, inactive, or published. Fill in whether you have data on the disk to be uploaded in the “Data to be uploaded to Allas or Databank?” column. Document the backup status of your drive to the “Data backed up” column
Organise your data according to the folder structure guideline below (Section 5.1). 
Update the project and README log files
Ownerless external disks will be emptied and the data will be moved immediately.

f your drive to the “Data backed up” column
Organise your data according to the folder structure guideline below (Section 5.1). 
Update the project and README log files
Ownerless external disks will be emptied and the data will be moved immediately.
5 Folder structure reorganization - prioritize files going to Allas/Databank - update folder structure to match guidelines below:
Report to Deb the data you have that must be uploaded to Allas (active projects) or Databank (published or inactive).
5.1 GUIDELINE (folders name might vary depending on the project, still try to maintain similar structure)
Project_name (project number +_name)
│ 
│
├── README.txt (responsible person+date)
│ 
│ 
├── notebooks (if needed) 
│ 
│ 
├── raw_data 
│ 
│ 
├── intermediate_data
│ 
│ 
├── results 
│ 
│ 
└── scripts
7 Upload all of your conference materials to Google Drive - Including abstracts, poster files, prepared slides for speeches, photos etc. + make a meeting memo highlighting the main points presented in the conference 
Instructions:Guidelines for conference abstracts and reporting
Folder for materials: Conference materials
7 Network drive data mapping to standardize data structures - prioritize active projects:
L-drive - 
P-drive H304 (Cell cycle data only) - Ada, Nika
P-drive H345 - Joonas (iPDC), Inga-Maria, Maria, Aleksandra, Iga, Hanna, Zhihan, Wenqing, Sara, Ada
P-drive H30492 - Ziqi, Ada, Nika, Hanna, Zhihan, Iga, Aleksandra
➡️
↘️
Specific Guidelines for network drives: 
For personal projects/data - first list all your data in your personal disk. Categorize them properly - active/inactive(archived)/published. Data that are still in use should be keppt moved Make a document listing everything, add notes if necessary, and share with Deb.

/data - first list all your data in your personal disk. Categorize them properly - active/inactive(archived)/published. Data that are still in use should be keppt moved Make a document listing everything, add notes if necessary, and share with Deb. 
For shared (project) data folders - make a group of 2-3 members with enough experience in lab research projects.
Identify active, inactive, and published data.
List all the projects/data as active, and/or inactive/archived in a document and share the document with Deb. Make sure the data path given is correct. 
Delete all files and folders that are no longer necessary (Please make sure the data is no longer needed).
List also the files that have been deleted (for cross-check) (optional).

vector_chunks.source_file: Cleaning day 251205/Dry lab cleaning day personal to do list.xlsx

vector_chunks.text:
Wet lab cleaning day
8:00–12:00 am - Aino & Saun
12:00–15:00 am - Aino
People expected to join the wet lab cleaning: María, Sarah, Kürşat
We hope people can join around 9:00 am. If possible, help with point 1, but we especially need assistance with points 2 and 3:
2. Discuss and agree on the most convenient way to organize cold spaces
3. Help with transferring cells into LN boxes and then into the LN tank
Action points:
General wet lab cleaning
Ensure overall cleanliness of the wet lab space - tidying, not major reorganization unless needed
Discard expired items (chemical cupboard, shelves).
Ensure all chemicals have an ID code written by Saun.
Wipe down all surfaces.
Check shelf organization and reorganize if necessary.
Label shelves and drawers for improved visibility.
Fridge & freezer organization 
Organize the following fridges and freezers: 
Main lab fridge/freezer
Cell culture fridge
Lola’s room freezer
-80 freezer: especially omentum gel box
Discard expired items.
Assign shelves for specific purposes where possible (e.g., new arrivals stored in the same location).
Cell to liquid nitrogen (LN)
Transfer cells to LN.
Help with transferring cells to LN boxes and then to the LN tank.
Chemical registry
Update the chemical registry.
Complete waste forms and discard empty containers.
Lab Coats
Return unused lab coats.
Send coats not in active use for washing.
